tldr: Council deferred most business items to March 2, 2026 to allow more time for discussion on contentious issues including temporary elimination of development charges. The meeting heard competing delegations on development charges relief from builders' groups and residents opposed to the proposal, as well as concerns about heritage property designation and council governance policies.

topics.name: Strong Mayor's Powers and information gathering
Code of Good Governance and Staff Relations Policies
Heritage Response to Bill 23 Phase 2 - Property Designation

decisions.item: Motion to waive rules of procedure to allow late delegations (David Barker - DGM-326, Shannon Gillies - heritage, Lynn Crosby - DGM-306)
Motion to defer entire business agenda (excluding proclamations, recognitions, and delegations) to March 2, 2026 special council meeting
Motion to approve amended agenda (with deferred items)
Motion to approve regular council meeting minutes from January 26, 2026
Deferral of development charges agenda item to March 2 council meeting, with referral back to Committee of the Whole for robust discussion
Go into closed session to discuss confidential items
Confirm confidential committee of the whole closed meeting minutes of February 9
Confirm confidential closed meeting minutes (item with Councillor Galbraith conflict)
Enact and pass Bylaw 11-2026 to confirm proceedings of council meeting of February 17, 2026

delegations.speaker: Michael Collins Williams
delegations.summary: CEO of West End Home Builders Association representing 300+ member companies advocated for immediate temporary elimination of development charges, stating the housing pipeline is seized and jobs are being lost daily. He disputed revenue loss projections and emphasized federal and provincial support for municipal housing action, noting only 4 condo units sold in Burlington in 2025.

delegations.speaker: Mike Moffat (HPA - Home Builders' Professional Association)
delegations.summary: Advocated for a 2-year temporary elimination of development charges to address housing crisis and construction job losses, arguing charge reduction would be passed to consumers and generate long-term property tax growth. Recommended a cap around $1.8 million to focus on workforce housing.

delegations.speaker: Jim Thompson
delegations.summary: Opposed eliminating development charges, arguing it transfers costs to property taxpayers and will result in inflated future property taxes. He stated the core issue is structural (wage-to-housing cost disparity) not cyclical, and proposed instead deferring charges until certificate of occupancy and 75% occupancy.

delegations.speaker: Jim Thompson
delegations.topic: Code of Good Governance and Staff Relations Policies
delegations.summary: Noted that 80% of city policies have not been reviewed in a timely manner and identified outdated Enterprise Risk Management and IT Security policies. He criticized staff for failing to provide timely reports and requested thorough review of council-staff relations policies per Municipal Act obligations.

delegations.speaker: Shannon Gillies
delegations.topic: Heritage Response to Bill 23 Phase 2 - Property Designation
delegations.summary: Objected to her property at 1419 Ontario Street being ranked #2 for heritage designation, arguing it is not at demolition risk and property owners received insufficient notice. She requested removal from the list or deferral and advocated for proactive voluntary designation encouragement rather than forced designation.

delegations.speaker: Shannon (resident at 1419 Ontario Street)
delegations.topic: Heritage Designation and Restrictions on Property Modifications
delegations.summary: Expressed concerns about future accessibility modifications to her heritage property while neighbouring identical properties lack such constraints. She preferred heritage character area designation as an alternative to full heritage designation to achieve more equitable treatment.

delegations.speaker: David Barker
delegations.summary: Strongly opposed eliminating development charges, arguing it would transfer $16-41 million in lost revenue to property taxpayers via higher taxes. He criticized developers for ignoring zoning while seeking subsidies and proposed instead deferring charges until certificate of occupancy and 75% occupancy.

delegations.speaker: Lynn Crosby
delegations.topic: Development Charges Elimination - Process and Democratic Concerns
delegations.summary: Criticized the rushed process and lack of citizen engagement on development charges, noting the matter was released before Family Day weekend limiting public awareness. She opposed waiving charges entirely, raised concerns about strong mayor powers, and questioned reliance on uncertain provincial/federal funding.

bylaws.title: Bylaw to confirm the proceedings of council at meeting of February 17, 2026

key_issues.summary:
The meeting's central issue was a proposal to temporarily eliminate development charges to address the housing crisis and construction industry job losses. Industry delegations from the West End Home Builders Association and Home Builders' Professional Association advocated for a 2-year exemption or elimination, arguing that this would relieve pressure on housing prices and stimulate the stalled construction pipeline. However, multiple residents (Jim Thompson and David Barker) opposed the measure, contending it would transfer substantial costs ($16-41 million in lost revenue) to existing property taxpayers through future tax increases, and that the core issue is structural rather than cyclical. Lynn Crosby added procedural concerns about rushed decision-making and the use of strong mayor powers without adequate public engagement. Given the contentious nature and split council vote (4-3) on deferring business, Council chose to delay the development charges decision to a special March 2 meeting to allow Committee of the Whole to conduct more robust discussion.

A secondary issue involved heritage property designation at 1419 Ontario Street, where resident Shannon Gillies objected to her home being ranked #2 for heritage designation study. She cited concerns about unequal application of restrictions (adjacent identical properties not designated), insufficient notice, and potential future accessibility challenges. She expressed willingness to voluntarily designate her property after 5-6 years but opposed forced designation. Additionally, Jim Thompson raised governance concerns about outdated council policies, noting that 80% of city policies have not been timely reviewed in accordance with Municipal Act obligations, particularly council-staff relations policies.
